content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 13,
1993
William R.
Seligmann
Campbell City Attorney
Dempster, McDonald,
Seligiuann
and Raineri
20380 Town Center Lane, Suite 215
Cupertino,
CA
95014
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-349
Dear Mr.
Seligmann:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice with
respect to the duties of Campbell City Councilmember Robert
Dougherty under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
May Councilmember Dougherty participate in decisions
affecting the zoning and building requirements in the San Tomas
Study area,
which will also affect his own residence?
CONCLUSION
So long as the components of the study proposal will affect
the councilmember in substantially the same manner as it will
affect the rest of the study area, the “public generally”
exception would apply and the councilmember may participate.
Where a decision will affect the councilmember materially,
and will not affect a significant segment of the jurisdiction in a
similar manner, the councilmember may not participate in that
decision.
However,
he may still participate in all other aspects
of the plan so long as:
(1)
the decision for which the
councilmeinber has a disqualifying financial interest is segregated
from the other decisions;
(2)
the decision for which the
councilinember is disqualified is considered first,
and a final
decision is reached by the city council without his participation.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93-349
Page
2
(3)
Once a decision has been made on the portions of the plan for
which the counciliuember has a disqualifying interest, the
deliberations regarding other portions of the plan will not result
in a reopening or in any way affect the decision from which he was
disqualified.
FACTS
The city is currently conducting a study of the San Tomas
Area of the city.
The study will deal with a variety of issues,
including the permitted size of homes and lots in the area, the
sufficiency of existing setbacks and building heights,
design and
use restrictions of parcels in the area,
and the improvement of
streets and traffic flow.
You stated that the San Tomas Area has a population of 10,685
and covers a land area of 1.5 square miles.
This constitutes 29
percent of the total city population of 36,478,
and 23 percent of
the city’s 6.3 mile land area.
The area is primarily residential.
Councilmember Dougherty owns a single family residence in the
study area.
You have asked whether the councilmember may
participate in the San Tomas Study or the future implementation
decisions concerning the study.
In your letter of September 13,
1993,
you stated that the
study has been completed and the city council will now be
considering specific proposals with respect to the study area.
You described the following proposals:
(1)
front, rear and side
yard setbacks;
(2)
building coverage (floor area);
(3)
maximum
building height;
(4)
minimum lot width;
(5)
front yard paving;
(6)
extensions along building lines;
(7)
accessory buildings;
(8)
landscaping;
(9) site and architectural review;
(10)
planned
development zones;
(11)
general plan/zoning amendments; and,
(12)
transportation issues.
You stated that Councilmember Dougherty owns a single family
home in the study area in a R—1-8 zoning district.
In addition,
you stated that his residence is in compliance with most of the
proposed changes, with the exception of the lot width requirement.
ANALYSIS
As we discussed in our prior letter,
Section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making,
or otherwise using the official’s position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any real
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect
interest worth $1,000 or more.
(Section 87103(b).)


Our File No.
A-93-349
Page
3
Thus, the councilmember is prohibited from participating in
or influencing any decision concerning the study area if the
decision will have a foreseeable
2 and material financial effect on
his home.
Rezoning in the Study Area
The materiality of a financial effect on real property is
measured according to the standards set forth in Regulations
18702.1 and 18702.3.
Regulation 18702.1 provides that where a
councilmember’s real property is directly involved in a decision,
the effect of the decision is deemed to be material and
disqualification is required.
Where a governmental decision concerns zoning or rezoning,
and a public official resides in the zone, the effect of the
decision is deemed material and the official may not participate.
3
(Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (A).)
Thus,
under your facts, the
councilmember would be disqualified from participating in the
decisions if they will have any financial effect on the value of
his home.
However, Regulation 18703 provides an exception to the
conflict—of-interest provisions if the effect on the official’s
interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally.
For the “public generally” exception to apply,
a
decision must affect the official’s interests in substantially the
same manner as it would affect a significant segment Of the
public.
(Regulation 18703.)
On September 7,
1993, the Commission adopted specific
standards to determine when a governmental decision will fall
within the “public generally” exception.
New Regulation 18703
provides that the “public generally” exception applies where both
subdivisions
(1)
and
(2)
apply.
(1)
Significant Segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment” of the
public generally as set forth below:
(A)
The decision will affect:
2
Whether the financial effects of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(.Ii
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
Regulation 18702.1(a) (3) (E)
defines “zoning” decisions to
exclude amendments to an existing zoning ordinance which are ap
plicable to all properties designated in that category.
However,
the zoning changes you have described are limited to the study
area and therefore would not fall within this exception.


Our File No.
A—93-349
Page 4
(i)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents, or
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners, or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents, or
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents,
so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade, or
profession; or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
***
(2)
Substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the official’s
economic interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision
(a) (1)
of this
regulation.
You stated that more than 29 percent of the population of the
city live in the study area.
To the extent that the decisions in
question will affect the entire study area in substantially the
same manner
(or enough of the study area to equate to 10 percent
of the city’s population), the “public generally” exception would
apply.
It appears from your facts that the proposals regarding
setbacks and building coverage, while applying different standards
to the various zoning categories in the area, will in fact affect
the majority of the zoning categories in substantially the same
manner.
For example, with respect to the front yard setbacks, the
councilmember’s zoning category
(R-l-8) will be subject to the
same standards as R—l—6 and R—l—9 zoned properties.
With respect
to side yard setbacks, the councilmember’s zoning category
(R—l—8)
will be subject to the same standards as R-l-9,
10 and 16 zoned
properties.
Additionally,
you stated that even where the
standards did differ,
you did not believe that the degree of
difference would result in any of the properties being affected
differently by the amendments.
Moreover, with respect to maximum building height and minimum
lot width,
and the rules regarding front yard paving, extensions
along building lines, accessory buildings,
and landscaping--these


Our File No.
A—93-349
Page 5
standards will be applied uniformly throughout the study area.
Thus,
so long as there are no facts to indicate that any specific
component of the study proposal will affect the councilmeinber in a
manner that differs from the rest of the study area, the “public
generally” exception would apply.
Traffic Decisions
With respect to the traffic and street decisions proposed for
the study area,
4 Regulation 18702.3 provides in pertinent part:
(a)
The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an official has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
following applies:
(1)
The real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within a 300 foot
radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official’s real property interest.
(2)
The decision involves construction
of,
or improvements to, streets, water,
sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities,
and the
real property in which the official has an
interest will receive new or substantially
improved services.
Regulation 18702.3 (a).
Thus, pursuant to Regulation 18702.3(a) (1),
where a decision
regarding streets affect streets adjacent to or near the
councilmember’s residence, the effect is considerédmaterial if
there will be any financial effect on the councilmeiuber’s property
or if the councilmember’s property will receive new or
substantially improved services.
Moreover, unlike the general zoning decisions discussed
above, where the councilmember’s residence will be materially
affected by a decision concerning a street near his property, the
change would generally not affect a significant segment of the
jurisdiction in the same or similar manner as it would affect the
councilmember.
Thus, the “public generally” exception would not
apply.
Regulation 18702.3 would apply to all decisions that affect the
councilmember’s property in a manner that is not considered
“direct” under Regulation 18702.1
(copies enclosed).


Our File No.
A-93-349
Page
6
However,
as we discussed previously,
large,
complex decisions
may be divided into separate decisions under certain circumstances
so that when an official has a disqualifying interest in one
component of the series of decisions,
he may still participate in
the other components in which he has no financial interest.
(Merkuloff Advice Letter,
No.
1-90-542; Huffaker Advice Letter,
No.
A—86—343.)
If you determine that a specific street decision will affect
the councilmember’s residence materially,
he may still participate
in all other aspects of the plan so long as:
(1) the decision for
which the councilmember has a disqualifying financial interest is
segregated from the other decisions;
and
(2)
the decision for
which the councilmember is disqualified is considered first,
and a
final decision reached by the city council without his
participation.
Once a decision has been made on the portions of
the plan for which the councilmember has a disqualifying interest,
the councilmember may participate in the deliberations regarding
other portions of the plan,
so long as those deliberations do not
result in a reopening or in any way affect the decision from which
he was disqualified.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, supra.)
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—590l.
Enclosures
Sincerely,
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10c per page.
Steven G.
Churchwell
ra1 Counsel
Legal Division

disqualified.

QUESTION: May Councilmember Dougherty participate in decisions
affecting the zoning and building requirements in the San Tomas
Study area,
which will also affect his own residence?

CONCLUSION: So long as the components of the study proposal will affect
the councilmember in substantially the same manner as it will
affect the rest of the study area, the “public generally”
exception would apply and the councilmember may participate.
Where a decision will affect the councilmember materially,
and will not affect a significant segment of the jurisdiction in a
similar manner, the councilmember may not participate in that
decision.
However,
he may still participate in all other aspects
of the plan so long as:
(1)
the decision for which the
councilmeinber has a disqualifying financial interest is segregated
from the other decisions;
(2)
the decision for which the
councilinember is disqualified is considered first,
and a final
decision is reached by the city council without his participation.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.

which the counciliuember has a disqualifying interest, the
deliberations regarding other portions of the plan will not result
in a reopening or in any way affect the decision from which he was
disqualified.
